title: Atomic Structure
subtitle: Chapter 3: Structure of Atom — Complete NEB Notes

content.text: 1. Introduction to Atomic Structure
Atom is the smallest particle of an element that retains its chemical properties. Modern atomic theory is based on the discovery of subatomic particles: Electron, Proton, and Neutron.
2. Subatomic Particles
Comparison of Fundamental Particles:
Electron (e⁻): Discovered by J.J. Thomson (1897). Mass = 9.1 × 10⁻³¹ kg = 0.00055 u. Charge = -1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C = -1 unit.
Proton (p⁺): Discovered by Goldstein (1886). Mass = 1.672 × 10⁻²⁷ kg ≈ 1 u. Charge = +1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C = +1 unit.
Neutron (n⁰): Discovered by Chadwick (1932). Mass = 1.675 × 10⁻²⁷ kg ≈ 1 u. Charge = 0 (neutral).

content.label: 💡 Quick Tip
content.text: Mass of proton ≈ Mass of neutron ≈ 1837 × Mass of electron. Electron is the lightest subatomic particle.
3. Atomic Number (Z) and Mass Number (A)
Atomic Number (Z): Number of protons in the nucleus. For neutral atom: Z = number of protons = number of electrons.
Mass Number (A): Total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. A = Z + N, where N = number of neutrons.

content.name: Relation
content.formula: A = Z + N \quad ; \quad N = A - Z

content.text: 4. Isotopes, Isobars, Isotones
Isotopes: Atoms of same element (same Z) with different mass numbers (different A). Due to different number of neutrons. Examples: ¹H, ²H, ³H (Hydrogen isotopes); ¹²C, ¹³C, ¹⁴C.
Isobars: Atoms of different elements (different Z) with same mass number (same A). Examples: ₁₈⁴⁰Ar and ₂₀⁴⁰Ca; ₂₇⁵⁹Co and ₂₈⁵⁹Ni.
Isotones: Atoms with same number of neutrons (same N) but different Z and A. Examples: ₆¹³C (N=7) and ₇¹⁴N (N=7); ₁₅³¹P (N=16) and ₁₆³²S (N=16).

content.label: 🎯 Memory Trick
content.text: Isotopes → Same place (Z) in periodic table • Isobars → Same weight (A) • Isotones → Same neutrons (N)
5. Thomson's Atomic Model (Plum Pudding Model, 1904)
J.J. Thomson proposed that atom is a sphere of positive charge with electrons embedded in it like plums in a pudding (or seeds in a watermelon).
Postulates: • Atom is electrically neutral • Positive charge and mass are uniformly distributed • Electrons are embedded to balance positive charge

content.label: ❌ Drawback
content.text: Could not explain Rutherford's α-scattering results. Failed to account for stability of atom and discrete spectral lines.
6. Rutherford's α-Scattering Experiment (1911)
Experimental Setup: α-particles from radioactive source (Po/Ra) → Lead block (collimator) → Thin gold foil (~10⁻⁷ m) → Zinc sulphide (ZnS) screen (produces flash when α-particle hits)
6.1 Observations
1. Most α-particles passed through gold foil without deviation → Atom has large empty space.
2. Some α-particles deviated by small angles → Positive charge is concentrated in a small region.
3. Very few α-particles (1 in 8000) bounced back (180°) → Positive charge and mass are concentrated in a very small volume called nucleus.
6.2 Conclusions (Nuclear Model)
Nucleus: Small, dense, positively charged central core containing protons and neutrons. Size ~10⁻¹⁵ m (vs atom size ~10⁻¹⁰ m).
Electrons: Revolve around nucleus in circular orbits. Atom is electrically neutral (Z protons = Z electrons).
Empty Space: Most of the atom is empty space.
6.3 Drawbacks of Rutherford's Model

❌ Stability Problem: According to classical EM theory, accelerating electron (in circular motion) should radiate energy, spiral inward, and fall into nucleus → Atom should collapse (but atoms are stable).
❌ Spectrum Problem: Should produce continuous spectrum, but hydrogen gives line spectrum (discrete wavelengths).
❌ No explanation for electron distribution or quantization of energy.
7. Bohr's Atomic Model (1913) — For Hydrogen-like Atoms
Niels Bohr combined Rutherford's nuclear model with Planck's quantum theory to explain hydrogen spectrum.
7.1 Postulates of Bohr's Model
1. Stationary Orbits: Electrons revolve in fixed circular orbits (stationary states) without radiating energy. Each orbit has fixed energy → Called energy levels/shells (K, L, M, N... or n=1,2,3...).
2. Angular Momentum Quantization: Only those orbits are allowed for which angular momentum is integral multiple of h/2π.

content.name: Quantization Condition
content.note: m = electron mass, v = velocity, r = radius, h = Planck's constant (6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s), n = principal quantum number

content.text: 3. Energy Emission/Absorption: Energy is emitted or absorbed only when electron jumps between orbits. Energy of photon: ΔE = E₂ - E₁ = hν = hc/λ

content.name: Photon Energy
content.note: ν = frequency, λ = wavelength, c = speed of light (3×10⁸ m/s)

content.label: ⚡ Key Point
content.text: Electron absorbs energy to jump to higher level (excited state). Emits photon when falling to lower level. Energy difference determines color/wavelength of light.
7.2 Success of Bohr's Model
✅ Explained stability of hydrogen atom • ✅ Calculated radius & energy of nth orbit • ✅ Successfully explained hydrogen line spectrum • ✅ Derived Rydberg formula theoretically

content.name: Energy of nth Orbit (Hydrogen)
content.note: Negative sign indicates bound state. E₁ = -13.6 eV (ground state). E∞ = 0 (ionization)

content.text: 8. Hydrogen Spectrum & Spectral Series
When hydrogen gas is excited (electric discharge), it emits light of specific wavelengths. Passing through prism gives line spectrum (not continuous).

content.name: Rydberg Formula
content.note: R_H = Rydberg constant = 109,677 cm⁻¹ ≈ 1.097×10⁷ m⁻¹; n₂ > n₁; λ = wavelength of emitted light

content.text: 8.1 Spectral Series of Hydrogen

1. Lyman Series: n₁=1, n₂=2,3,4... → UV region • 2. Balmer Series: n₁=2, n₂=3,4,5... → Visible region (Hα=656nm red, Hβ=486nm blue-green) • 3. Paschen Series: n₁=3, n₂=4,5,6... → IR region • 4. Brackett Series: n₁=4, n₂=5,6,7... → Far-IR • 5. Pfund Series: n₁=5, n₂=6,7,8... → Far-IR

content.label: 🎨 Visible Lines
content.text: Only Balmer series is visible to human eye. Lyman is UV (harmful), others are IR (heat). Remember: Lyman = Lowest (n=1), Balmer = Beautiful (visible)
8.2 Energy Level Diagram
Energy levels get closer as n increases. Ionization energy of H = 13.6 eV (energy to remove e⁻ from n=1 to n=∞). Transition from higher→lower emits photon; lower→higher absorbs photon.
9. Drawbacks of Bohr's Model

❌ Only works for hydrogen & hydrogen-like ions (He⁺, Li²⁺) — fails for multi-electron atoms • ❌ Could not explain fine structure of spectral lines (splitting in magnetic/electric fields: Zeeman & Stark effects) • ❌ Contradicts Heisenberg's uncertainty principle (assumes definite path) • ❌ Did not explain chemical bonding • ❌ No justification for angular momentum quantization (ad-hoc assumption)
10. Dual Nature of Matter — De Broglie Hypothesis (1924)
Louis de Broglie proposed that matter (like light) has dual character: particle nature and wave nature. Moving particles exhibit wave properties.

content.name: De Broglie Wavelength
content.note: λ = wavelength, h = Planck's constant, p = momentum, m = mass, v = velocity. For macroscopic objects, λ is negligible; for electrons, λ is significant.

content.text: Derivation: From Einstein: E = mc²; From Planck: E = hν = hc/λ → mc² = hc/λ → λ = h/mc. For particle with velocity v: λ = h/mv

content.label: 🔬 Application
content.text: Electron microscope works on wave nature of electrons (λ much smaller than light → higher resolution). Davisson-Germer experiment (1927) confirmed electron diffraction → proved wave nature.
11. Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle (1927)
It is impossible to determine simultaneously the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of a microscopic particle like electron.

content.name: Mathematical Form
content.note: Δx = uncertainty in position, Δp = uncertainty in momentum, h = Planck's constant. Product of uncertainties has minimum value h/4π.

content.text: Implications: • Concept of definite electron path (orbit) is meaningless → replaced by 'orbital' (probability region) • Significant only for microscopic particles (negligible for macroscopic objects)

content.label: 💭 Conceptual
content.text: If we know position very accurately (Δx small), momentum becomes highly uncertain (Δp large), and vice versa. This is fundamental property of nature, not measurement limitation.
12. Quantum Mechanical Model — Orbitals
Orbital: Three-dimensional region around nucleus where probability of finding an electron is maximum (≥90%). Replaces Bohr's 'orbit' (definite path).
Orbital vs Orbit: Orbit = definite circular path (Bohr, incorrect) • Orbital = probability cloud (quantum model, correct)
12.1 Shapes of Orbitals

s-orbital (l=0): Spherical, non-directional, size increases with n. One s-orbital per shell. • p-orbital (l=1): Dumb-bell shaped, three orientations (pₓ, pᵧ, p_z), directional. • d-orbital (l=2): Double dumb-bell/cloverleaf, five orientations (dₓᵧ, dᵧz, d_zₓ, dₓ²₋ᵧ², d_z²).

content.label: 🎨 Visualization
content.text: s = sphere • p = dumbbell (3 mutually perpendicular) • d = cloverleaf (4) + one with doughnut (d_z²). Nodes increase with energy.
13. Quantum Numbers
Four quantum numbers describe the complete state of an electron in an atom:
1. Principal Quantum Number (n): Indicates main energy shell/level. n = 1,2,3... (K,L,M...). Determines size and energy of orbital. Max electrons in shell = 2n².
2. Azimuthal Quantum Number (l): Indicates subshell/shape. l = 0 to (n-1). l=0→s, l=1→p, l=2→d, l=3→f. Determines orbital angular momentum: √[l(l+1)]·h/2π.
3. Magnetic Quantum Number (mₗ): Indicates orientation in space. mₗ = -l to +l (including 0). Number of orbitals in subshell = (2l+1).
4. Spin Quantum Number (mₛ): Indicates electron spin direction. mₛ = +½ (↑, clockwise) or -½ (↓, anticlockwise). Two electrons per orbital must have opposite spins.

content.name: Quantum Number Summary
content.note: Example: For 2p orbital: n=2, l=1, mₗ = -1,0,+1 (three 2p orbitals), each holds 2 e⁻ with opposite spins.

content.text: 14. Rules for Electronic Configuration
1. Aufbau Principle: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy. Order: 1s→2s→2p→3s→3p→4s→3d→4p→5s→4d→5p→6s→4f→5d→6p→7s... (Remember: (n+l) rule; if same (n+l), lower n fills first).
2. Pauli's Exclusion Principle: No two electrons in an atom can have same set of all four quantum numbers. → Maximum 2 electrons per orbital with opposite spins.

3. Hund's Rule of Maximum Multiplicity: Electron pairing in degenerate orbitals (same energy: p,d,f) does not occur until each orbital has one electron with parallel spins. → Maximizes total spin, minimizes repulsion.

content.label: ✏️ Writing Config
content.text: Write in order of increasing n, not filling order: e.g., Fe (26): 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d⁶ (not 3d⁶ 4s²). Noble gas shorthand: [Ar] 4s² 3d⁶
15. Exceptions in Electronic Configuration
Half-filled (d⁵, f⁷) and fully-filled (d¹⁰, f¹⁴) subshells are extra stable due to symmetry and exchange energy.
Chromium (Cr, Z=24): Expected: [Ar] 4s² 3d⁴ • Actual: [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵ (half-filled d-subshell more stable)
Copper (Cu, Z=29): Expected: [Ar] 4s² 3d⁹ • Actual: [Ar] 4s¹ 3d¹⁰ (fully-filled d-subshell more stable)

content.label: 🎯 Memory Aid
content.text: Cr & Cu steal one electron from 4s to make d-subshell half/full. Similar exceptions: Mo (42), Ag (47), Au (79).
16. Important Formulas Summary

content.label: 🎓 Exam Tips
content.text: • Always convert nm to m (×10⁻⁹) or cm (×10⁻⁷) for Rydberg formula • Ionization energy = |E₁| = 13.6Z² eV • For multi-electron atoms, energy order: ns < (n-1)d < np • Degenerate orbitals: same n & l (e.g., three 2p orbitals) • Nodal planes: s=0, p=1, d=2
17. Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Using Bohr model for atoms other than H, He⁺, Li²⁺ • ❌ Forgetting negative sign in energy formula (indicates bound state) • ❌ Confusing orbit (path) with orbital (probability region) • ❌ Writing electronic configuration in filling order instead of n-order • ❌ Missing exceptions: Cr, Cu, Mo, Ag • ❌ Using °C instead of K in energy calculations (rare but possible) • ❌ Assuming all spectral series are visible (only Balmer is)
